coredata.dc:title: Construction of delayed fluorescence / phosphorescence dual-mode carbon dots and its application in paper anti-counterfeiting systems
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Hua, Xiangfeng
Jing, Yi
Ni, YeYan
coredata.dc:description: Printing encrypted messages and security labels using stimuli-responsive luminescent materials can no longer meet the requirements of protecting high-level confidential information. In this paper, carbon dots (CDs) with near-infrared emission potential at low temperatures were prepared by thermal polycondensation using citric acid as a carbon source and urea as a nitrogen source in a polysuccinimide (PSI) matrix. The prepared CDs@PSI achieved visible (yellow-green) and near-infrared (NIR) dual-mode afterglow emission at room temperature. The C=N covalent bond and hydrogen bond formed during the thermal polycondensation of CDs and PSI, as well as the crystal structure, multiple fixations, and rigidity effect forming during the slow cooling process of PSI are crucial to the activation of bimodal afterglow. By comparing with the samples prepared by the room temperature co-crystallization method, it is found that the effective regulation of the energy gap value can promote the simultaneous generation of intersystem interference and anti-intersystem interference, which is beneficial to RTP and TADF emission behavior. Under direct visual conditions, NIR-RTP is completely covered by yellow-green TADF and can be captured by a camera loaded with a filter with a cut-off wavelength of 700 nm. The enhancement effect of subfluorescent groups (C=O/C=N) on the surface of CDs with PSI polymer crystal bonds is the main reason for the visible light-activated afterglow. Based on the fact that two different afterglow emissions of a single excitation light source and visible afterglow can hide NIR afterglow coding information, a dual-mode delayed fluorescence/phosphorescence anti-counterfeiting system was constructed, and an anti-counterfeiting strategy for visible afterglow to hide NIR afterglow coding information was developed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thermal polycondensation
Dual-mode afterglow
Near-infrared emission
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of delayed fluorescence/phosphorescence dual-mode afterglow materials
Characterization and analysis of optical properties of RCDs @ PSI
The morphology and structure characterization of RCDs @ PSI
The dual-mode afterglow mechanism of RCDs @ PSI
Multi-mode anti-counterfeiting encryption application based on UV-visible dual-mode excitation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The RCDs-PVA film was irradiated with a 395 nm ultraviolet lamp under sunlight, and photographed under environmental conditions after the lamp was turned off without using and using an optical filter (cut-off wavelength (λ Cut-off) was 700 nm).
Schematic diagram of PSI synthesized by l-Asp, the schematic diagram of RCDs embedded in PSI matrix in situ, and the possible structure of RCDs @ PSI.
Images of the RCDs@PSI powder under 500 nm irradiation (a) and LED light (b), their afterglows without and with an optical filter (cut-off wavelength (λCut-off) of 700 nm) being used.

(a) Normalized UV–Vis absorption, photoluminescence excitation (λEM = 591 nm), fluorescence emission, and phosphorescence emission (λEX = 492 nm) spectra of RCDs @ PSI powder (Abs: absorption; eX / EM: excitation/emission); (b) Under 500 nm excitation, the afterglow emission spectra of RCDs @ PSI powder were delayed by 0.1 ms and 50 ms at room temperature; (c) Afterglow emission spectra of RCDs @ PSI powder at different temperatures (TADF: thermally activated delayed fluorescence; rTP: room temperature phosphorescence); (d) The afterglow decay spectra of RCDs @ PSI powders were monitored at 550 nm and 750 nm under 500 nm excitation.
UV–Vis absorption, PL emission and excitation spectra of the RCDs dispersion in water.
(a) Low temperature (77 K) fluorescence (black line) and phosphorescence (red line) emission spectra of RCDs. (b) Fluorescence and phosphorescence emission spectra of RCDs and RCDs@PSI powder.
(a) TEM, and (b) HRTEM images of RCDs. (c) TEM, and (d) high resolution TEM (red area) images of the RCDs@PSI.
XRD patterns of pPSI, ePSI, and RCDs@PSI (pPSI: pure PSI, purchased from the commercial source; ePSI: experimental PSI, prepared from l-Asp by thermal condensation polymerization).
XPS spectra of RCDs@PSI (a) and (d) ePSI. High resolution XPS spectra and the corresponding fitting curves of C 1 s of RCDs@PSI (b) and (e) ePSI, N 1 s of RCDs@PSI (c) and (f) ePSI.
Photographs of RCDs#PSI powder under daylight, and irradiation with a 500 nm lamp on and after the lamp was just switched off.
Photographs of the afterglow emission of RCDs@PSI water dispersion with the 500 nm lamp of irradiation on and off.

Schematic illustration of the encryption and decryption procedure using the RCDs@PSI in information security applications. RCDs@PSI and CCDs@PSI as anti-counterfeiting ink and interference ink, separately, they are printed on commercial A4 paper by silk screen printing. (a) Excitation with 365 nm ultraviolet light; (b) Use a flashlight with LED light beads to excite.
PL quantum yields (QYs) of RCDs@PSI composites prepared by different ratios of RCDs and l-Asp.

row.entry: The ratio of RCDs to l-Asp (w /w,%)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Afterglow lifetimes of the RCDs@PSI powder at different temperatures (the afterglow lifetime was detected at 550 nm and 750 nm, respectively).
Compares the raw materials and afterglow emission types of NIR-RTP materials with those previously reported.

row.entry: iridium(III) complexes based on benzo-annulated N-linked corrole analogue
cyclometalated iridium complex bearing perylene diimide (PDI) ligand
diiodo-borondipyrromethene dye (I-2-BODIPY) encapsulated in hydrophobic polymers (Polysulfone, polyphenylene sulphide and fibrous polypropylene)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Relative contents of C, N and O atoms for RCDs@PSI and ePSI.

head.ce:title: Construction of delayed fluorescence / phosphorescence dual-mode carbon dots and its application in paper anti-counterfeiting systems

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources, International Innovation Center for Forest Chemicals and Materials, College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources, International Innovation Center for Forest Chemicals and Materials, College of Light Industry and Food Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources, International Innovation Center for Forest Chemicals and Materials, College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Printing encrypted messages and security labels using stimuli-responsive luminescent materials can no longer meet the requirements of protecting high-level confidential information. In this paper, carbon dots (CDs) with near-infrared emission potential at low temperatures were prepared by thermal polycondensation using citric acid as a carbon source and urea as a nitrogen source in a polysuccinimide (PSI) matrix. The prepared CDs@PSI achieved visible (yellow-green) and near-infrared (NIR) dual-mode afterglow emission at room temperature. The C=N covalent bond and hydrogen bond formed during the thermal polycondensation of CDs and PSI, as well as the crystal structure, multiple fixations, and rigidity effect forming during the slow cooling process of PSI are crucial to the activation of bimodal afterglow. By comparing with the samples prepared by the room temperature co-crystallization method, it is found that the effective regulation of the energy gap value can promote the simultaneous generation of intersystem interference and anti-intersystem interference, which is beneficial to RTP and TADF emission behavior. Under direct visual conditions, NIR-RTP is completely covered by yellow-green TADF and can be captured by a camera loaded with a filter with a cut-off wavelength of 700 nm. The enhancement effect of subfluorescent groups (C=O/C=N) on the surface of CDs with PSI polymer crystal bonds is the main reason for the visible light-activated afterglow. Based on the fact that two different afterglow emissions of a single excitation light source and visible afterglow can hide NIR afterglow coding information, a dual-mode delayed fluorescence/phosphorescence anti-counterfeiting system was constructed, and an anti-counterfeiting strategy for visible afterglow to hide NIR afterglow coding information was developed.

In situ embedding and fixing RCDs into PSI matrix the encryption and decryption procedure using the RCDs@PSI in information security applications.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:list-item.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, various anti-counterfeiting technologies and methods, such as bar code, digital watermarking, radio frequency identification, laser holography, and fluorescence anti-counterfeiting, have been widely used to protect precious and important items [1-4]. However, single-mode ultraviolet (UV) excited fluorescence (FL) patterns are more and more likely to be imitated by illegal counterfeiters [5-7].

As a new type of carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have excellent optical properties, good biocompatibility, and low toxicity [8-10]. They can be used to replace semiconductor quantum dots, rare earth luminescent materials, and organic luminescent materials, and are expected to become a new generation of ' green ' luminescent materials. Its photoluminescence phenomena, including room temperature phosphorescence (RTP), thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF), and long afterglow luminescence (LPL), show broad application prospects in the field of anti-counterfeiting. The mechanism and preparation of the fluorescence properties of CDs have been widely studied. However, compared with phosphorescent materials, the short fluorescence lifetime of CDs limits the wide application of CDs [11-14].

Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials based on carbon dots (CDs) have attracted more and more attention in the fields of optoelectronic devices, photocatalysis, food packaging, anti-counterfeiting, information encryption, and bioimaging due to their excellent optical properties, low biological toxicity, and easy preparation [15-19].In particular, their long afterglow characteristics and dual-mode technology are more difficult to copy or imitate, thus showing a more excellent anti-counterfeiting effect.

Thermally Activated Delayed Fluorescence (TADF) is gaining increasing attention as a type of long-lasting luminescence. It efficiently captures 100 % of excitons, resulting in high luminescence efficiency [20]. TADF is typically achieved through reverse intersystem crossing (RISC) of singlet excitons from triplet (T*) to singlet (S*) excited states. By utilizing a combination of the 2D space/electron confinement effect and a moderate-heavy atom tuning strategy, it is possible to minimize the non-radiative decay of excitons. This can be achieved by using organic-metal halide perovskite materials and leveraging the rigid layered structure of perovskite. This approach holds promise for achieving a high blue light quantum yield and an extremely long TADF life at room temperature and pressure. Recent research has found that tunable multicolor afterglow based on novel metal-organic halide mono/dinuclear complexes can be achieved by enhancing and balancing the RTP or TADF emission states. By adjusting the coordination mode, molecular stacking, and electronic structure between metal halides and organic ligands, it is expected to develop a series of similar metal-organic coordination compounds for multicolor afterglow and photonic information applications [21].

The molecular ultralong room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) has garnered significant attention because of its potential applications in light-emitting devices, sensors, and information security, owing to its multiple stimuli-responsive properties. A recent study has discovered that the bismuth (Bi)–based metal-organic halide (MOH) can result in continuous and light-responsive ultra-long luminescence RTP. This discovery can be applied to multi-mode anti-counterfeiting and information encryption[22].Metal-organic halides (MOHs), which can provide spatial-temporal-color resolution, use photon signals as the main information carrier to ensure information security [23]. One-dimensional (1D) metal-organic halides (MOHs) exhibit dynamic ultra-long RTP and photochromic optical waveguides, obvious visual contrast, color-time-space multi-dimensional adjustable ultra-long RTP, showing multimode luminescence signal output, and its information storage capacity is significantly higher than that of typical fluorescence systems, expanding frontier photonic applications such as micro / nano-level multi-level data encryption and information storage[24]. The model system chosen was room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) carbon dots (CDs) highly dispersed on g-C3N4. These CDs exhibited significantly enhanced photocatalytic and electrochemical activity compared to the typical fluorescent CD system.

Although the encryption performance has been improved, the afterglow based on single-mode visible light still lacks sufficient confidentiality [25-26]. In addition, the development of near-infrared long afterglow materials is of great significance in the commercialization and industrial application of anti-counterfeiting labels, because the near-infrared light source has the advantages of strong concealment (invisible to the naked eye) and transparent biological windows [27-29]. Due to the invisibility and insensitivity of near-infrared (NIR) light to human vision, hiding NIR-encoded information in visible afterglow may be an ideal encryption strategy. Unfortunately, materials with this property are difficult to prepare, and few reports have been published.

There are two primary methods for creating carbon dot-based room-temperature phosphorescent materials. The first involves introducing heteroatoms and creating a rigid environment through space confinement. Hydrogen bonds can help provide this rigid environment for carbon dots by inhibiting the vibration and rotation of the groups, which promotes the emission of room-temperature phosphorescence [30].

However, compared with hydrogen bonds, covalent bonds are stronger and more conducive to the excitation of afterglow. Multiple fixations of the triplet state through covalent bonds, hydrogen bonds, and physical constraints, such as rigid networks and nano matrix spaces, are more effective in activating the afterglow emission of CDs. This multi-restriction effect greatly limits the non-radiative decay of the triplet excited state, enabling the CD composite material to obtain RTP (room-temperature phosphorescence) properties [31-33].

If CDs with potential near-infrared (NIR) afterglow properties are doped in a suitable matrix to create CDs-based bimodal afterglow emission, both RTP and TADF (thermally activated delayed fluorescence) bimodal afterglow can be achieved. It's worth noting that most CDs-based composites are currently only excited by ultraviolet light, which greatly limits their practical use [29-30]. Therefore, developing carbon dot-based room-temperature phosphorescent materials with dual-mode afterglow emission excited by visible light remains a significant challenge [34-37].

In this work, carbon dots-based room temperature phosphorescent materials with dual-mode afterglow emission were successfully prepared by using citric acid as a carbon source and urea as a nitrogen source in a polysuccinimide (PSI) matrix.. This work opens up a possibility for the development of dual-mode afterglow luminescent nanomaterials based on CDs for anti-counterfeiting.

Citric acid monohydrate (99.8 %), urea (>99.5 %), l-aspartic acid (L-Asp, 99%) and poly succinimide (named pPSI, 98 %) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. Sodium hydroxide (≥98%, anhydrous), ammonia (NH3
H2O, 25% −28%), anhydrous ethanol (> 99.9 %) and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF 99.5 %) were purchased from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ultrapure water was supplied by the Ultrapure Water System (EU-LS-100TF, Nanjing).

First, 4 g citric acid and 8 g urea were added to 40 mL N, N-dimethylformamide for ultrasonic treatment for 20 min until completely dissolved. The mixture was then transferred to a 100 mL stainless steel reactor lined with Teflon and reacted at 160 °C for 6 h. The red-brown reaction solution was collected, and 80 mL NaOH (50 mg/mL) was added and stirred violently. The mixture is dissolved in deionized water and centrifuged at 16,000 °C for 10 min to remove residual salt and alkali. The dark products obtained after freeze-drying were named RCDs.

2 mL of ammonia water was completely dissolved in 8 mL of deionized water, and 1.5 g l-Asp was added to the mixed solution. The mixture was sonicated for 10 min until the precipitate was completely dissolved. Subsequently, the mixed solution is transferred to a glass beaker and covered with a tin paper cover to prevent excessive evaporation of water vapor. The glass beaker covered with tin paper was placed in a blast drying oven and heated at 200 °C for 1 h to completely evaporate the water. The crude product was dispersed in 80 mL N, N-dimethylformamide, stirred at 40 °C for 2 h, filtered to remove the insoluble, and then a certain amount of anhydrous ethanol was added to the filtrate for precipitation. The solid obtained by precipitation was collected, washed with distilled water to neutral, and freeze-dried, and the obtained solid was named ePSI.

To prepare composite materials with RCDs, various amounts of RCDs (0.75, 1.875, 7.5, 15, 37.5, and 75 mg) were completely dissolved in 8 mL of deionized water. Then, 2.0 mL of ammonia and 1.5 g of l-Asp were slowly added during the stirring process until a clear solution formed. The mixed solution was covered with a tin paper cover and transferred to a blast-drying oven. The mixture was then subjected to high-temperature consolidation treatment at different temperatures (30, 100, 150, 200, 250, and 300 °C) until the water in the reaction system completely evaporated. The obtained reddish-brown honeycomb solid was dispersed in 80 mL of N, N-dimethylformamide, slowly stirred at 40 °C for 2 h, filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove insoluble material, and then a certain amount of anhydrous ethanol was added to the filtrate for precipitation. The solid obtained by precipitation was collected, washed with distilled water to neutralize, and freeze-dried. The obtained solid was named RCDs @ PSI.

RCDs #PSI was made using the co-crystallization method. To start, 0.5 g of pPSI was mixed in N, N-dimethylformamide and ultrasonicated until it dissolved completely. Then, 50 mg of RCDs powder was added to the mixture and ultrasonicated until it dissolved. The resulting mixture was transferred to a glass beaker, covered with tin foil, and left to solidify at room temperature for 10 h. The crystals formed were washed with anhydrous ethanol to remove DMF on the surface, then neutralized with distilled water, and finally freeze-dried to obtain a solid named RCDs #PSI.

The microscopic morphology of CDs was observed by transmission electron microscopy (JEM-1400, Japan) and high-power transmission electron microscopy (JEM-2100 UHR, Japan). The microstructure of phosphorescent materials was observed by cold field emission scanning electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) measurements were carried out in the range of 400∼4000 cm−1 using a VERTEX 80 V spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed on an Ultima IV spectrometer (Rigaku, Japan). XPS testing with AXIS UltraDLD Spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The Lambda 950 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer Medical Diagnostics Shanghai Co., Ltd., USA) was used to test its absorption spectrum. Fluorescence/phosphor spectra, excitation spectra, fluorescence/phosphor intensity, and fluorescence/phosphor lifetime were recorded using an FLS 1000 steady-state/transient spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK) equipped with a 150 W xenon lamp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of delayed fluorescence/phosphorescence dual-mode afterglow materials
ce:section.ce:para: To verify whether hydrogen bonding fixation could activate the afterglow emission of RCDs, RCDs were embedded in the PVA polymer matrix in this study. As shown in Fig. 1 ., the RCDs-PVA film was a lavender transparent film under sunlight.

At room temperature, the RCDs-PVA film was exposed to 395 nm ultraviolet light, causing the film to emit red luminescence. However, once the UV light was turned off, no afterglow emission was observed from the RCDs-PVA film. To confirm that PVA alone could not activate the near-infrared afterglow properties of RCDs, a filter with a cutoff wavelength of 700 nm was attached to the lens during filming. As a result, the afterglow phenomenon of the RCDs-PVA film could not be captured, indicating that hydrogen bonding fixation alone was insufficient to activate the afterglow properties of RCDs.

In this study, the researchers immobilized RCDs within a PSI matrix through thermal polycondensation. As illustrated in Fig. 2 , PSI was generated in situ using l-Asp as the host matrix, with PSI crystals formed during a gradual cooling process. This in situ process fixed RCDs to create RCDs @ PSI. To achieve optimal PL performance, the researchers screened different proportions of RCDs and l-Asp to prepare RCDs @ PSI.

To achieve the best photoluminescence (PL) performance, Composites of rare-earth coordination polymers (RCDs) with high phosphorescence quantum efficiency by using different ratios of RCDs and l-Asp were prepared. The results indicated that the afterglow quantum yield increased as the amount of RCDs relative to the mass of l-Asp increased. At a dosage of 0.5 %, the phosphorescence quantum efficiency reached its maximum. However, further increasing the number of RCDs led to a gradual decrease in the afterglow quantum yield of RCDs @ PSI, suggesting that a high RCD content could result in high concentration annihilation. This information is summarized in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and analysis of optical properties of RCDs @ PSI
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 3(a) , the image showed the state of RCDs @ PSI powder under 500 nm and LED light irradiation. When exposed to 500 nm excitation light, the RCDs @ PSI powder emitted bright white fluorescence. Subsequently, upon removal of the excitation light, a yellow-green afterglow was visible from the RCDs @ PSI powder. This afterglow was easily discernible to the naked eye and lasted for a few seconds. A lens equipped with a filter with a cutoff wavelength of 700 nm was used to capture NIR afterglow emissions of more than 5 s. This signified the successful development of the composite material exhibiting the NIR afterglow phenomenon.

It was interesting to note that the afterglow of RCDs @ PSI powder could be activated by visible light, as shown in Fig. 3(b). When an LED lamp(white light)was used to irradiate the RCDs @ PSI powder, it turned orange-red in color. Upon removal of the LED light source, the naked eye could still observe the yellow-green afterglow. Moreover, a camera equipped with a filter having a cutoff wavelength of 700 nm could capture the near-infrared afterglow. It could be seen that the afterglow characteristics obtained by using visible light to excite RCDs @ PSI powder were consistent with those obtained by using a 500 nm excitation light source.

The visible light activation of RCDs was due to the interaction between the subfluorescent groups (CO and CN) on the RCDs' surface and the PSI polymer. A covalent bond (CN) was formed between the RCDs and PSI, firmly attaching the RCDs to the surface of the PSI. The structure of the polymer and the crystal structure of PSI restricted the vibration and rotation of the subfluorescent groups inside the RCDs, promoting effective radiation transition. Additionally, the wide half-peak width of the PL excitation peak of RCDs @ PSI resulted in more overlapping areas with the UV absorption spectrum, making the afterglow of the synthesized RCDs @ PSI more easily excited by visible light.

In the afterglow spectra depicted in Fig. 4(b) at various delay times, it was noted that at a delay time of 1 ms, the afterglow spectrum exhibited two peaks at approximately 550 nm and 754 nm. As the delay time was increased to 50 ms, the peak at 754 nm disappeared from the afterglow spectrum, while the peak at 550 nm showed little change. By using a filter with a cutoff wavelength of 700 nm, it was possible to capture a near-infrared afterglow of about 750 nm with a camera. This indicates that the prolonged afterglow lifetime at around 550 nm and the near-infrared emission at about 750 nm, which is not visible to the naked eye, may contribute to the direct observation of yellow-green afterglow by the naked eye.

The steady-state photoluminescence (PL) and short-wavelength afterglow emissions from RCDs @ PSI showed clear similarities. It was initially hypothesized that the two peaks of afterglow emissions were due to Thermally Activated Delayed Fluorescence (TADF) (λmax=550 nm) and Room Temperature Phosphorescence (RTP) (λmax=750 nm) of RCDs @ PSI, respectively. To test this hypothesis, the researchers studied the temperature-dependent afterglow emission characteristics of RCDs @ PSI powder.

As shown in Fig. 4(c), as the temperature gradually increases from 298 K to 498 K, the afterglow intensity at 550 nm gradually increases, while the intensity at 750 nm gradually decreases. It shows that the afterglow peak at 550 nm is TADF, and the peak at 750 nm is RTP. To further verify the TADF at 550 nm and the RTP at 750 nm, the afterglow lifetime of the two emission sites under different temperature conditions was also monitored. As shown in Table 2 , as the temperature increased, the afterglow lifetime of the 550 nm monitoring point gradually increased. On the contrary, the afterglow lifetime of the 750 nm monitoring point gradually decreased. This phenomenon further proved that the afterglow emission at 550 nm and 750 nm was attributed to TADF and RTP, respectively.

Table 3 lists the previous research progress of NIR-RTP materials. As known from the data in the table, to prepare NIR-RTP materials, researchers usually use transition metal complexes as composite raw materials. At the same time, the materials prepared by using transition metals only have NIR-RTP single afterglow emission. The RCDs @ PSI material prepared in this research has double afterglow emission of TADF and NIR-RTP, which expands its application range in the optical field.

To prepare NIR-RTP materials, researchers usually use transition metal complexes as composite raw materials. At the same time, the materials prepared by using transition metals only have NIR-RTP single afterglow emission. The RCDs @ PSI material has double afterglow emission of TADF and NIR-RTP, which expands its application range in the optical field. The afterglow decay spectra of RCDs @ PSI were shown in Fig. 4(d).RTP and TADF showed the decay processes of double-exponential and triple-exponential functions, respectively. Under the excitation wavelength of 500 nm, the average lifetime of TADF at 550 nm was 234.4 ms, and the average lifetime of NIR-RTP at 750 nm was 20.6 ms. This is consistent with the afterglow spectra of different delay times in Fig. 4b.

Based on the formula ΔEST = ET-ES = 1240 / λem, FL-1240 / λem, Phos (ΔEST represented the energy gap between singlet excited state ES and triplet excited state ET), the low-temperature FL and Phos spectral energy gap of RCDs was calculated to be 0.472 eV, and the energy gap of RCDs @ PSI is 0.453 eV (Fig. 6 ). The energy gap of RCDs @ PSI powder was smaller than that of free RCDs. RCDs only demonstrate very weak phosphorescence emission at low temperatures of 77 K, which might be due to their large energy gap and ineffective ISC. The reduction of the energy gap of RCDs @ PSI proves vital in activating the dual-mode afterglow of RCDs @ PSI, as compared to RCDs.

In general, a smaller ΔEST was beneficial to the effective intersystem crossing (ISC) process to promote RTP emission, but too small ΔEST would lead to an effective reverse intersystem crossing (RISC) process to make TADF dominant. Therefore, adjusting the ΔEST value to an appropriate range was essential for the simultaneλous generation of RTP and TADF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The morphology and structure characterization of RCDs @ PSI
ce:section.ce:para: To verify that RCDs have been successfully embedded in the PSI matrix, the morphology and structure of RCDs and RCDs @ PSI were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 6(a), RCDs e were spherical nanoparticles with uniform distribution and no obvious agglomeration, with a diameter of about 7–8 nm.

The HRTEM image of RCDs in Fig. 7 (b) showed that RCDs had a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the graphite (100) surface. To confirm the successful embedding of RCDs into PSI, the TEM and HRTEM images of RCDs @ PSI were studied. As shown in Fig. 7(c), RCDs @ PSI did not dissolve in water, but maintained a block structure. The particle state of RCDs could not be observed from the TEM images, but clear lattice fringes corresponding to RCDs could be found from their HRTEM images (Fig. 7d), and the shape of these lattice regions was elliptical and close to circular, with diameter of about 5 nm, indicating that RCDs have been successfully embedded in PSI crystals. The diameter of this lattice region was slightly smaller than that of the original RCDs, which might be due to the decrease of some groups on the surface of RCDs during high-temperature thermal polycondensation.

In addition, the crystallization states of RCDs @ PSI, pPSI (purchased from commercial sources), and ePSI (prepared by thermal polycondensation of l-Asp) were analyzed. It was observed from Fig. 7 that the XRD patterns of pPSI and ePSI were similar, showing the same characteristic peaks but differing in peak intensity. The results demonstrated that the process of producing high-quality PSI crystals through thermal polycondensation was both practical and dependable. Additionally, the PSI crystals prepared with the addition of RCDs displayed the same diffraction peak position as those prepared without RCDs. It suggested that regardless of the presence of RCDs, the thermal polycondensation of l-Asp consistently yielded fine PSI crystals.The intensity of some XRD diffraction peaks of RCDs @ PSI was stronger than that of PSI, which could be attributed to the fact that RCDs embedded in PSI crystals will affect the accumulation and crystal orientation of PSI molecules.To clarify the existence form of RCDs in the PSI matrix, XPS measurements were performed on PSI and RCDs @ PSI, as shown in Fig. 9.

The XPS-based composition analysis shown in Table 4 showed that RCDs @ PSI and ePSI were mainly composed of the same elements (i.e., carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen). The increase of carbon content in RCDs @ PSI indicated that RCDs were successfully embedded in PSI. In addition, the C1s and N1s high-resolution spectra of RCDs @ PSI and ePSI were fitted, as shown in Fig. 9. For PSI, the C1s spectrum could be divided into three peaks at 284.7, 286.2, and 288.5 eV, corresponding to CC, CN, and CO, respectively). The N1s spectrum could only be divided into one peak at 400.1 eV, corresponding to CN (Fig. 9f). The peak separation of the XPS spectrum of RCDs @ PSI showed that the peak separation of C1s was consistent with that of PSI, while the peak separation of N1s showed that in addition to CN at 400.1 eV, a new peak appeared at 401.6 eV. This peak might be due to the formation of a new covalent CN bond during the in-situ embedding of RCDs and PSI (Fig. 8
c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The dual-mode afterglow mechanism of RCDs @ PSI
ce:section.ce:para: The covalent bond formed between the CDs and the matrix led to a decrease in the energy gap, which was a possible reason for the dual-mode afterglow emission of the corresponding composites. To verify this hypothesis, a complex of RCDs and PSI (named RCDs#PSI) was prepared by co-crystallization. According to the relative content of different functional groups in pPSI and RCDs#PSI listed in Table 4, the functional groups of the composite were not significantly different from those of pPSI, indicating that RCDs were fixed in PSI through non-covalent interactions.

As shown in Fig.10 , there was no afterglow in RCDs # PSI, indicating that the hydrogen bonds or physical constraints between RCDs and PSI were not sufficient to activate the afterglow of RCDs. In contrast, the RCDs#PSI prepared by in-situ embedding RCDs into the PSI matrix by thermal polycondensation introduced a covalent bond between RCDs and PSI, providing an additional and more effective immobilization effect for the triplet state of RCDs. Therefore, the covalent bond formed between RCDs and PSI was considered to be crucial for the dual-mode afterglow of RCDs @ PSI. In addition, RCDs @ PSI also exhibited dual-mode afterglow emission in an aqueous solution similar to that in powder state (Fig. 11 .), but compared with solid-state, TADF and NIR-RTP performance was significantly reduced in the dispersed state. It indicated that the hydrogen bond confinement effect also contributes to the fixation of the triplet state of RCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-mode anti-counterfeiting encryption application based on UV-visible dual-mode excitation
ce:section.ce:para: Compared with NIR-RTP, the yellow-green TADF emission peak intensity of RCDs @ PSI was relatively weak. Only yellow-green TADF could be seen directly due to the short lifetime of NIR-RTP and the fact that human vision is not sensitive to NIR. When capturing the afterglow image of RCDs @ PSI using a camera, only the yellow-green afterglow could be captured. However, adding a filter with a cut-off wavelength of 700 nm to the camera lens enabled the afterglow of NIR-RTP to be easily captured. RCDs @ PSI had a unique afterglow characteristic and high stability which made them suitable for use in information security applications such as anti-counterfeiting encryption.

Two types of inks - RCDs @ PSI and CCDs @ PSI - were developed for use as anti-counterfeiting and interference inks, respectively. A simple screen printing method was used to print lotus flowers and leaves on commercial A4 paper using these inks, as shown in Fig.12a . The paper surface contained a strong fluorescent whitening agent, which made it difficult to distinguish the printed pattern with the naked eye under the excitation of a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp in PL mode. However, when the excitation light source was turned off, the whole lotus leaf pattern showed yellow-green long-life luminescence in the afterglow mode. Even under camera capture, only yellow-green luminescence could be observed.

After adding a filter with a cut-off wavelength of 700 nm to the camera lens, only the lotus part emitted NIR light in the afterglow mode. To verify the anti-counterfeiting mode based on the characteristic that the afterglow of RCDs @ PSI material could be activated by visible light, a flashlight equipped with LED beads was used as the excitation light source (Fig.12b). When using a flashlight to illuminate the A4 commercial paper with an anti-counterfeiting pattern, the naked eye cannot detect the pattern. However, upon turning off the flashlight, a yellow-green afterglow is observed in the lotus part in the dark. This is due to the application of RCDs @ PSI on the lotus part, and the persistent afterglow can be activated by visible light. The lotus leaf portion was coated with ChCDs @ PSI and did not respond to visible light. Similarly, the near-infrared afterglow emitted by the lotus part could be captured by a camera lens equipped with a filter with a cut-off wavelength of 700 nm.

In situ embedding of RCDs into poly(succinylacetamide) (PSI) substrates using a thermal polycondensation method. RCDs@PSI achieve visible (yellow-green) and near-infrared (NIR) dual-mode afterglow emission from TADF and RTP, respectively, under ambient conditions with optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of 492 nm and 754 nm, respectively.

Morphological and structural characterization of RCDs@PSI revealed the successful synthesis of PSI and the in situ embedding of RCDs. The multiple fixation and rigidity effects of CN covalent bonds formed between RCDs and PSI during thermal polycondensation, hydrogen bonds, and the crystal structure formed by PSI during slow cooling are essential for activating the bimodal afterglow.

The visible light activation is attributed to the enhancement effect between the subfluorescent groups (CO and CN) on the surface of RCDs and PSI polymers. First, CN covalent bonds are formed between the RCDs and PSI, firmly attaching the RCDs to the PSI surface. Then the polymer structure and crystal structure of PSI itself fixes and restricts the vibration and rotation of the subfluorescent groups inside RCDs, promoting effective radiation leap. The half-peak width of the PL excitation peak of RCDs@PSI is large, and the overlapping region with the UV absorption spectrum is large.

Since the NIR-RTP of RCDs@PSI has a short lifetime and is insensitive to the naked eye, the NIR-RTP is completely covered by the yellow-green TADF in the case of direct observation. However, by filtering the emitted light with a filter with a cut-off wavelength of 700 nm, the NIR-RTP can be captured by the camera equipped with the filter. Based on this special property, an anti-counterfeiting strategy is developed to hide the NIR-RTP encoded information from the visible afterglow.
Xiangfeng Hua: Writing – original draft. Yi Jing: Supervision. YeYan Ni: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No.31370583).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Achieving purple light excitable high-efficiency temperature-responsive dual- and single-mode afterglow in carbon dots
